title: SEGMENT 10 — Getting the drug to the bug: pharmacokinetics in brief

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Getting the drug to the bug: pharmacokinetics in brief
Welcome back.

Before we dive into the ribosome, I want to spend a little time on something that, since this is a pharmacology course, I really can't ignore: it doesn't matter how brilliant a drug's mechanism is at its target if the drug can't physically get to where the bacteria are living, at a high enough concentration, for long enough.
A perfect mechanism delivered to the wrong place is a failure.
So let me give you the pharmacokinetic frame — how the drug moves through the body — and tie each piece back to mechanism and clinical decisions.
Pharmacokinetics is often summarized by four processes, sometimes abbreviated ADME: absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion.
Let's walk them with antibiotics in mind.
Absorption — getting the drug into the bloodstream.
The big practical question is oral versus intravenous.

Some antibiotics are absorbed beautifully from the gut and achieve blood levels nearly as high as if you'd injected them — these have high oral bioavailability, and that's wonderful because the patient can take a pill at home.
Many fluoroquinolones are like this; their oral and IV levels are so similar that you can often switch a hospitalized patient from the IV to the pill as soon as they're improving, and send them home.
Other drugs are barely absorbed orally and must be given by injection to treat a systemic infection — vancomycin is the classic example: give it by mouth and it essentially stays in the gut.

Now, here's a lovely mechanism-meets-pharmacokinetics twist: because oral vancomycin stays in the gut and isn't absorbed, that "limitation" becomes the treatment for an infection of the gut — C. difficile colitis — where you actually want the drug to sit in the intestine and not go anywhere else.
A pharmacokinetic weakness, repurposed as a strength by matching it to the right target location.
Distribution — where the drug goes once it's in the blood.
This is where antibiotic therapy gets genuinely hard, because some of the most important infection sites are places the body deliberately walls off, and the drug has to be able to reach them.

The brain and spinal fluid are guarded by the blood-brain barrier, which keeps many drugs out — so to treat meningitis, you specifically need an antibiotic that penetrates into the cerebrospinal fluid, and not all of them do.
Bone is poorly perfused and hard to reach, which is part of why osteomyelitis — bone infection — requires prolonged therapy with drugs known to get into bone.
The prostate, the eye, the interior of an abscess — all are sanctuary sites with their own access problems.
So choosing an antibiotic isn't only "what's the right mechanism for this bug" — it's "what drug with the right mechanism will also actually reach the place this bug is hiding.

" And remember daptomycin from later in our list: a fine anti-gram-positive mechanism, but it gets inactivated in the lung, so distribution to the wrong chemical environment kills its usefulness for pneumonia.
Mechanism and distribution must both line up.
There's also the matter of getting inside cells.
Some bacteria live inside our own cells — the organisms behind diseases like the atypical pneumonias and chlamydia hide intracellularly.
To kill those, the antibiotic has to penetrate into human cells and accumulate there.
Macrolides and tetracyclines are good at this, which is exactly why they're favored for those intracellular pathogens.
A drug that stays in the bloodstream will never reach a bug that's hiding inside a cell.
Metabolism and excretion — how the body clears the drug, which determines the dose and the dosing interval, and which can dictate whether a drug is even safe in a given patient.

Many antibiotics are cleared by the kidneys, so in a patient with poor kidney function the drug accumulates and you must reduce the dose to avoid toxicity — this matters enormously for the aminoglycosides and vancomycin, which are themselves hard on the kidneys, so the very organ that clears them is the one they damage.
Other drugs are cleared by the liver.

And we already met two clinically huge metabolic interactions, both involving the liver's cytochrome P450 enzyme system: macrolides inhibit P450, slowing the clearance of many other drugs and raising their levels, while rifampin induces P450, speeding clearance and dropping the levels of other drugs, sometimes to the point of making them fail — including, famously, oral contraceptives and certain transplant drugs.
So the antibiotic doesn't just act on the bacterium; it can reshape how the patient handles everything else in their medicine cabinet.
Now let me tie this back to the killing patterns, because pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics meet here, and this is the synthesis.
Recall: beta-lactams are time-dependent — you want the level above the MIC for as much of the interval as possible.
The pharmacokinetic consequence is frequent dosing or continuous infusion, and a preference for drugs with a longer half-life so the level doesn't crash between doses.

Aminoglycosides are concentration-dependent with a long post-antibiotic effect — the pharmacokinetic consequence is one big daily dose to drive a towering peak, then let the level fall, relying on the post-antibiotic effect to cover the long trough, which conveniently also gives the kidneys a daily rest from the drug, reducing toxicity.

So once-daily aminoglycoside dosing simultaneously maximizes killing and minimizes toxicity — a beautiful case where understanding both the mechanism and the pharmacokinetics lets you design a dosing scheme that's better on both axes at once.
That is pharmacology doing exactly what it's supposed to do.
So as we go through the rest of the drug classes, keep a second question running alongside "what's the mechanism?
" — namely, "can this drug get to where it needs to be, at the right level, for the right amount of time?
" Because the bacteria don't care how elegant the mechanism is if the drug never shows up.
Now — the ribosome.
